metadata.title: The Core Failure Mode (And Why Your Draft Probably Has It)

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Hitting the word count doesn't mean the idea is strong; a vague central claim is the core failure mode. A weak claim like 'poor data quality leads to bad performance' names no actor, number, or consequence. A strong claim specifies who deploys what, the measurable harm, and who bears it. Your work must map to an existing framework or justify itself as genuinely novel infrastructure.

artifactLines: Hitting the word count doesn't mean the idea is strong; a vague central claim is the core failure mode.
A weak claim like 'poor data quality leads to bad performance' names no actor, number, or consequence.
A strong claim specifies who deploys what, the measurable harm, and who bears it.
Your work must map to an existing framework or justify itself as genuinely novel infrastructure.

beats.narration_text: I keep hitting the word count while my central claim stays vague, like the 'poor data quality leads to bad performance' example. I want to feel the difference between a weak claim and the strong sepsis version that names hospitals, the 30% miss, and the demographic harm. Take a soft claim from my own field and (1) rewrite it into a specific, consequence-bearing version, (2) show me which existing framework it maps onto or why it's novel infrastructure, and (3) list the three details that made it stop being generic.
